checks.skill: classify a limiting factor as density-dependent vs density-independent by mechanism
checks.question: A disease spreads faster and kills a larger fraction of a population as the population gets more crowded. Is this density-dependent or density-independent, and why?
checks.answer: Density-dependent — its per-capita effect rises with N (more individuals means more contact and faster transmission), so the factor intensifies as the population grows denser.
checks.misconception_tested: Classifying by memorized example bins instead of asking whether the per-capita effect changes with population size.

checks.skill: identify exponential vs logistic growth from a described scenario
checks.question: A bacterial population is placed in a fresh nutrient broth with effectively unlimited food and no competitors, and it doubles every 20 minutes. Which growth model best fits, exponential or logistic?
checks.answer: Exponential growth (J-curve, dN/dt = rN) — resources are briefly unlimited and the population has just colonized, so growth accelerates without a carrying-capacity brake yet.
checks.misconception_tested: Defaulting to logistic because it is 'more realistic,' ignoring that the scenario specifies unlimited resources and fresh colonization.

checks.skill: match a species/strategy to a survivorship curve type
checks.question: Oysters release millions of larvae, almost all of which die young, with only a tiny fraction surviving to adulthood. Which survivorship curve type and which life-history strategy is this?
checks.answer: Type III survivorship (steep early mortality, then it flattens) paired with an r-selected strategy (many offspring, little parental care).
checks.misconception_tested: Confusing Type III (early die-off, r-selected) with Type I (late mortality, K-selected), or treating curve type and strategy as unrelated.

checks.skill: interpret carrying capacity K in the logistic model
checks.question: A deer population briefly rises above K, then declines back toward it over the next few years. Does exceeding K mean K was measured wrong?
checks.answer: No. K is the long-run level the environment can sustain, not a hard wall. Real populations can overshoot K and then oscillate or crash back toward it because the (K - N)/K term goes negative above K, slowing or reversing growth.
checks.misconception_tested: Treating K as an absolute ceiling that can never be crossed, rather than the settling level the population corrects toward.

checks.skill: compute the sign of per-capita growth rate r including migration
checks.question: In a population, per-capita births = 0.10, deaths = 0.12, immigration = 0.05, emigration = 0.01 (per year). Is the population growing, shrinking, or stable?
checks.misconception_tested: Using births minus deaths alone (which gives -0.02, falsely suggesting decline) and forgetting to add immigration minus emigration.
